title: Practice problem - Chef and Happy String

content.problemStatement:
Chef has a string SSS with him consisting of only lowercase alphabets.

Chef is happy if the string contains a **contiguous substring** of length **strictly greater** than 222 in which all its characters are vowels. 

Determine whether Chef is happy or not. 

**Note**: in english alphabet, vowels are `a`, `e`, `i`, `o`, and `u`.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains of a single line of input, a string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, if Chef is happy, print `HAPPY` else print `SAD`.
constraints: The sum of |S| over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting only of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "HAPPY" if S has a contiguous substring of length strictly greater than 2 consisting only of vowels (a, e, i, o, u); otherwise output "SAD".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: "aeiou" contains "aei" (three consecutive vowels) → HAPPY.
Test case 2: No three consecutive vowels → SAD.
Test case 3: Vowels are isolated, never three in a row → SAD.
Test case 4: Substring "eea" is three vowels → HAPPY.
1) "aaa" itself is three vowels → HAPPY.\n2) No vowel runs of length ≥3 → SAD.\n3) The prefix "uioua" contains "ioua" which has a run of four vowels → HAPPY.
Both strings never reach three consecutive vowels, so both are SAD.

hidden.label: edge case - exactly three vowels at end

solution.approach: Traverse the string once while maintaining a counter of consecutive vowel characters. Whenever a non‑vowel is encountered, reset the counter to zero. If the counter ever becomes 3 (i.e., we have seen three vowels in a row), we can immediately conclude the answer is HAPPY for that test case. Otherwise, after scanning the whole string, the answer is SAD. This runs in O(|S|) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total length of all strings) – each character is examined at most once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
